b1.title: Block 1
b1.sub: M3 review + Advantage+ Audience vs original

cards.body: The audience you choose is not a detail separate from the budget: it's what eats it up. Every time your ad is shown to a person, you spend money, so the size and quality of your audience determine how fast and at what cost your investment is depleted. Meta's system needs to gather enough conversion events to exit the learning phase (the initial period in which the algorithm is still figuring out who is worth showing the ad to). That signal is only gathered if the audience is large enough for the budget to buy those events; if it's tiny, the money is spent without reaching the minimum and the cost per result never stabilizes. That's why it's worth reviewing the Module 3 budget before planning audiences: they go hand in hand.

Before moving on, keep three budget concepts fresh that reappear here. 1. Learning phase: the initial period in which Meta explores who to show the ad to; it needs around 50 optimization events in about 7 days to exit and stabilize. 2. Auction: the buying type in which the budget is estimated from the results you expect to get, not set exactly. 3. CBO / Advantage+ campaign budget: a setting where you place the money at the full-campaign level and the system distributes it on its own toward the best-performing ad set.

cards.h: Example: how distribution depends on size
cards.body: Imagine an online English academy called Fluentia that sets up a campaign with an Advantage+ campaign budget and three ad sets, each with a different audience. The system starts pushing spend toward the ad set that's getting cheaper conversions, but this only works if each audience is large enough to gather the minimum signal. If Fluentia had put a tiny audience in one of the three ad sets, that ad set would get stuck in the learning phase and the automatic distribution would end up discarding it. The lesson is direct: the budget doesn't perform on its own, it performs based on the audience you place under it.

cards.q: An ad set exits the learning phase with ~___ optimization events in ~7 days.
cards.explanation: ~50 events in ~7 days. An audience that's too small prevents gathering that signal.

cards.q: Budget in Auction is derived as:
options: Target results × expected cost per result
cards.explanation: In Auction it's estimated. The audience affects the actual cost per result.

cards.q: CBO distributes spend toward the ad set with the best ___.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ campaign budget sends spend to the best ad set/audience.

cards.q: Which budget does ad scheduling (dayparting) require?
cards.explanation: Lifetime budget. A Module 3 reminder before planning audiences.

lessons.title: Explain · Advantage+ Audience vs original audience

cards.body: For years, planning a campaign meant telling Meta exactly who to show the ad to: you defined age, location, and interests, and the system stayed within that wall. That changed. Today Meta recommends the Advantage+ Audience as the starting point, where you provide hints and the system decides who to reach. The idea is that the algorithm, fed by the conversion signal from your Pixel, finds better prospects than you could define by hand. It emerged because very fine manual targeting tends to leave out people who would have converted. In practice, you provide signals (audiences you contribute as a reference or starting point) and the system looks for whoever is most likely to convert, without being tied to a closed audience.

cards.h: What the signals you provide are
cards.body: The signals you give an Advantage+ Audience can be of two types. 1. Custom audiences: lists of people who already know your business, such as your website visitors or your customer base. 2. Detailed targeting (interests, behaviors, and demographics). The key difference from manual targeting is that here they don't work as a wall, but as a starting point that tells the system "start looking here." For example, a specialty coffee brand called Tostadero Norte provides its subscriber list and the interest "gourmet coffee" as a signal; Meta starts by prioritizing similar profiles, but if it detects buyers in another profile that wasn't on the list, it can move beyond it and show them the ad too.

cards.h: The original audience is still there
cards.body: The original audience (the classic manual targeting) did not disappear: it defines the audience with strict control and the system respects that limit to the letter. You use it when you can't afford to let the algorithm expand on its own: sectors with legal restrictions (for example, financial or health products that can only be advertised to certain profiles by law), brand restrictions, or very narrow audiences that must not grow. The key difference for the exam: in the original audience, what you define is a wall that isn't crossed; in Advantage+, it's a suggestion the system can go beyond. For example, an insurer called Norteña Seguros, which by regulation cannot show a certain product to minors, needs the original audience to guarantee that limit, not Advantage+.

cards.h: Where this is chosen in Ads Manager
cards.body: This decision is configured when building the ad set. In Ads Manager, go into your campaign, open the ad set and scroll down to the Audience section. There Meta shows you the Advantage+ Audience first as the default option; you'll see an Audience suggestions field to add your signals. If you need strict control, you choose the option to switch to the original audience (it usually appears as Switch to original audience options) and there you define age, location, interests, and exclusions as hard limits.

rows: Signals as a suggestion; Meta expands to find conversions
Targeting defined by the planner; strict control
Maximize results, prospecting, conversion signal available

cards.label: AUDIENCE SUGGESTIONS
cards.body: In Advantage+ Audience, the custom audiences and detailed targeting you add work as suggestions that prioritize, not as rigid limits. The system can go further if it finds conversions.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Believing that in Advantage+ Audience the signals are a hard limit. They are not: they prioritize, but the system can expand. If you need a real limit, use the original audience.

cards.q: An advertiser in a regulated sector must NOT show ads outside an audience strictly defined by law. What do you choose?
options: Advantage+ Audience, which is the default
cards.explanation: When there's a hard legal limit, you need strict control: original audience with defined targeting. Advantage+ Audience treats signals as suggestions and can expand outside the audience.

cards.q: A business with no restrictions and a solid conversion signal. Recommended default?
options: Advantage+ Audience with signals as a suggestion
cards.explanation: With signal and no legal limits, Advantage+ Audience is Meta's recommended starting point.

cards.q: The default Meta recommends as a starting point is ___.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Audience: you provide signals and the system finds who converts, without hard limits.

cards.q: When there are legal or brand restrictions and you need strict control, you use the ___ audience.
cards.explanation: The original (manual) audience gives defined targeting and strict control of the audience.

cards.q: In Advantage+ Audience, what role do the custom audiences and detailed targeting you add play?
options: They are rigid limits the system cannot cross
They are suggestions that prioritize, not limits; the system can go further
cards.explanation: In Advantage+ Audience the signals are suggestions that prioritize; the system can expand if it finds conversions.

cards.q: When is Advantage+ Audience a good fit?
options: When there's a hard legal limit on the audience
To maximize results and prospect with a conversion signal available
When the audience must be very narrow by brand
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Audience serves to maximize results and prospect when there's a conversion signal; legal/brand limits call for the original audience.

cards.q: If you need a real audience limit (not a suggestion), you use the ___ audience.
cards.explanation: Only the original audience imposes a hard targeting limit.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: An accounting firm that sells services to SMBs wants to generate more leads. It has a Pixel with Lead events and no legal restrictions on who to show ads to. The partner asks you which type of audience to start with.

cards.q: What do you recommend to start with?
options: Original audience with very tight targeting
Advantage+ Audience, giving it the Lead signals as a suggestion
cards.explanation: With no legal restrictions and a conversion signal (Lead), Advantage+ Audience is the recommended default: the signals prioritize and the system finds who converts.

cards.q: The partner fears Advantage+ Audience will "ignore" their target company list. What do you explain?
options: They're right, the signals are discarded
The custom audiences and detailed targeting act as suggestions that prioritize, they are not ignored
You must switch to the original audience no matter what
cards.explanation: In Advantage+ Audience the signals are suggestions that prioritize; the system uses them to focus and can expand if it finds conversions.

cards.q: If later the firm serves a regulated sector that prohibits reaching anyone outside a certain audience, what would you do?
options: Keep using Advantage+ Audience the same way
Switch to the original audience with defined targeting for strict control
cards.explanation: A hard legal limit requires strict control: original audience with defined targeting, because Advantage+ Audience can expand outside the audience.

cards.q: Advantage+ Audience
cards.a: Recommended default: you provide signals as a suggestion and Meta finds who converts, without hard limits.

cards.q: When the original (manual) audience?
cards.a: When there are legal/brand restrictions or strict control of the audience is needed.

cards.q: In Advantage+ Audience, are custom audiences a limit?
cards.a: No; they work as suggestions that prioritize, not as a rigid limit.

cards.q: When is Advantage+ Audience a good fit?
cards.a: To maximize results, prospect, and when a conversion signal is available.

cards.q: What imposes a real audience limit?
cards.a: Only the original (manual) audience.

cards.body: Detailed targeting is the way to narrow down who the ad reaches based on three criteria Meta combines from the data it has on each person. Interests are topics people interact with on the platform (cooking, fitness, travel); behaviors are detected actions or habits (frequent online shoppers, recurring travelers); demographics are traits such as age, education, or marital status. It mostly serves to build cold audiences, that is, people who don't yet know your business, when you don't have enough of your own signal yet (little Pixel history or no customer list). For example, a newly opened dental clinic called Sonrisa Plena, with no patient base yet, can start by targeting by demographics in its area and the interest "dental care" to get its first events.

cards.h: Refining the audience: narrow and exclude
cards.body: Once you define an interest, you can refine further with two tools that change the logic of the audience. 1. Narrow: adds a required condition with "AND" logic, so the person has to meet both criteria at once to be included. 2. Exclude: removes profiles with "NOT" logic, even if they meet the main interest. For example, a mountaineering gear store called Cumbre Gear can start with the interest "hiking," narrow with "AND also online shoppers," and exclude those who already follow its page so as not to spend on them. The goal is to refine, but carefully: every condition you add reduces the size of the audience, and an audience that's too small takes away the system's room to optimize.

Detailed targeting expansion is an option within the original audience that gives Meta permission to go beyond the interests you defined, but only if it improves results. It's a middle ground: you set the frame with your interests and the system can step outside it when it detects conversions beyond it. Don't confuse it with the Advantage+ Audience, where expansion is the default behavior and much broader; here it's an optional add-on built on top of a manual base that you decide to enable. For example, a supplements brand called VitaPro defines the interest "sports nutrition" but enables expansion so Meta can also reach similar people who convert, without VitaPro having to list every interest by hand.

cards.h: Where it's configured in Ads Manager
cards.body: All of this lives within the ad set, not in a separate section. In Ads Manager open your campaign, go into the ad set and, if you chose the original audience, scroll down to Detailed targeting: there you type the interest, behavior, or demographic and Meta suggests matches. The Narrow audience button adds the "AND" condition, and the Exclude people field removes profiles. The detailed targeting expansion checkbox appears below as an option to enable. While you edit, the estimated audience size meter on the right warns you if the audience got too small.

rows: Refine with AND/NOT to fine-tune the audience
Lets Meta go beyond the interest if it improves results (in the original audience)
Audience too small = high cost and little delivery

cards.label: BROAD PERFORMS
cards.body: With a good conversion signal, a broad audience tends to perform better than an over-segmented one: it gives the system room to find who converts.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: Over-segmenting (many interests + aggressive narrowing) creates tiny audiences: the system has no room to optimize and the cost spikes.

cards.q: A planner builds an audience with 8 nested interests and triple narrowing. Delivery is almost nil and the CPM is very high. Most likely cause?
options: The audience became too small to optimize
cards.explanation: Over-targeting shrinks the audience to a size where the system can't optimize; cost goes up and delivery drops. The solution is to broaden and let the signal work.

cards.q: When is detailed targeting most useful?
options: When you already have plenty of your own signal
For cold audiences without enough of your own signal
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting helps define cold audiences when there isn't yet your own signal for the system to use.

cards.q: Narrowing down by interests, behaviors, and demographics is called ___ targeting.
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting narrows by interests, behaviors, and demographics; useful for cold audiences without your own signal.

cards.q: To refine the audience with AND/NOT you use narrow / ___.
cards.explanation: Narrow / exclude refines with AND/NOT to fine-tune the audience.

cards.q: What is detailed targeting expansion for in the original audience?
options: It blocks any expansion of the audience
It lets Meta go beyond the interest if it improves results
It turns the campaign into remarketing
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting expansion lets Meta go beyond the defined interest if that improves results, within the original audience.

cards.q: What is the main risk of over-segmenting with many interests + aggressive narrowing?
options: The system optimizes better with fewer people
A tiny audience: the system has no room to optimize and the cost spikes
cards.explanation: Over-segmenting creates tiny audiences; with no room to optimize, the cost spikes and delivery drops. Broad audiences perform better with a good signal.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: Industrial distributor with no own signal
cards.body: A B2B industrial parts distributor in Apodaca is about to launch its first campaign. It has neither a Pixel with enough history nor a customer list. The team wants to build an audience with 9 nested interests and triple narrowing "so as not to waste money."

cards.q: What do you warn them about that plan of 9 interests + triple narrowing?
options: It's perfect, the more segmented the better
Over-segmenting creates a tiny audience: cost goes up and delivery drops
All that's missing is adding more interests
The problem is the creative, not the audience
cards.explanation: Over-targeting shrinks the audience to a size where the system doesn't optimize; cost goes up and delivery drops. It's better to broaden.

cards.q: Without enough of their own signal, what detailed targeting approach do you recommend to start?
options: A broader cold audience with a few relevant interests
Triple narrowing with all possible interests
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting serves cold audiences without your own signal; a broad audience with a few relevant interests is best to give the system room.

cards.q: They want Meta to be able to go beyond the interest if it performs better, while keeping the original audience. What do they enable?
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting expansion lets Meta go beyond the defined interest if it improves results, within the original audience.

cards.q: Detailed targeting
cards.a: Narrows by interests, behaviors, and demographics; useful for cold audiences without your own signal.

cards.q: Narrow / exclude
cards.a: Refines the audience with AND/NOT to fine-tune it.

cards.q: Detailed targeting expansion
cards.a: Lets Meta go beyond the interest if it improves results (in the original audience).

cards.q: Risk of over-targeting
cards.a: Audience too small: the system doesn't optimize, cost goes up and delivery drops.

cards.body: A custom audience is built with people who already have some relationship with your business, not strangers. Unlike detailed targeting, which starts from general interests Meta knows about everyone, here you start from your own signal: who visited your site, who used your app, your customer list, or who engaged with your content on Meta. It's the foundation of remarketing (reaching people who already know you again), because you're speaking to someone who has already taken a step toward you and therefore tends to convert more cheaply. For example, an online bookstore called Página y Tinta can build a custom audience of everyone who viewed a book's page but didn't buy it, to remind them a few days later with an ad.

cards.h: The four sources your people come from
cards.body: A custom audience draws from four possible sources, split between your own platforms and Meta's assets. 1. Website: visitors and events the Pixel logged, such as those who fired "AddToCart" when adding to the cart. 2. App activity: users and events inside your mobile application. 3. Customer list: your own base of emails or phone numbers, which you upload. 4. Engagement: people who watched your video, interacted with your Facebook or Instagram page, or opened an instant form. The first two and the list use your own data; engagement uses activity within Meta. For example, a restaurant with delivery service called Cocina del Barrio can create an Engagement custom audience of those who watched at least 15 seconds of its menu video.

A custom audience isn't only for remarketing; it serves three distinct functions in your audience plan, and it's worth being clear on them because the exam mixes them up. 1. Remarketing: reaching those who already know you again, for example to recover abandoned carts. 2. Seed: using it as a base to create a lookalike and prospect similar people who don't know you yet. 3. Exclusion: leaving it out of a campaign so as not to pay again for those who already converted. For example, a sneaker brand called PasoFirme uses its buyer list as a seed to prospect new customers and, at the same time, excludes that same list from the prospecting campaign so as not to spend again on those who already bought.

Custom audiences are built outside the ad set, in a dedicated section. In Ads Manager, open the main menu and go to Audiences; there click Create audience and choose Custom Audience. Meta will ask you to select the source (Website, App activity, Customer list, or Engagement) and define the rules, for example "visited this page in the last 30 days." Once created, that audience becomes available to use as targeting, as a lookalike seed, or as an exclusion when building any ad set.

rows: Pixel visitors / events (e.g., added to cart)
Watched a video, engaged with page/IG, opened an Instant form

cards.label: USE IN THE PLAN
cards.body: Custom audiences serve for remarketing (recovering carts) and as a seed for lookalikes. They're also excluded so as not to spend on those who already converted.

cards.label: PRIVACY
cards.body: The customer list is uploaded hashed (emails/phones protected): Meta does the match without exposing data in the clear. Uploading it unencrypted is a privacy error.

cards.q: The client wants to recover those who added to cart and didn't buy within 7 days. Which custom audience source?
options: Website custom audience by AddToCart event, excluding Purchase
cards.explanation: This is web-behavior remarketing: a Website custom audience with the AddToCart event, excluding those who fired Purchase. The buyers' customer list is exactly who you do NOT want to re-engage.

cards.q: An audience of people who already have a relationship with the business is called a ___ audience.
cards.explanation: Custom audience: website visitors, app users, customer list, or engagement.

cards.q: What are the four custom audience sources?
options: Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement
cards.explanation: The custom audience sources are Website, App activity, Customer list, and Engagement.

cards.q: A customer list is uploaded ___ (emails/phones protected) before the match.
cards.explanation: The customer list is uploaded hashed (emails/phones protected) for the match.

cards.q: Besides remarketing, what other two uses do custom audiences have?
options: Lookalike seed and exclusion of those who already converted
Defining the objective and the budget
cards.explanation: Custom audiences serve for remarketing, as a seed for lookalikes, and to exclude those who already converted.

cards.q: You want to stop spending on people who already bought. What do you do with a custom audience?
options: Use it as the main targeting
Exclude it from the prospecting campaign
cards.explanation: You exclude the buyers' custom audience so as not to spend again on those who already converted.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: An SMB that sells industrial uniforms online has a Pixel with AddToCart and Purchase events, plus a customer email base. Many users add to cart but don't buy within a week. The owner wants to recover them without paying again for those who already bought.

cards.q: What custom audience do you build to recover 7-day abandoned carts?
options: Website custom audience by AddToCart, excluding Purchase
cards.explanation: This is web-behavior remarketing: a Website custom audience with AddToCart, excluding those who fired Purchase.

cards.q: How do you use the customer email base in this plan?
options: Upload it hashed as a customer list and exclude it from the campaign so as not to re-engage buyers
Publish it unencrypted for a better match
Use it as the sole prospecting targeting
cards.explanation: The customer list is uploaded hashed and excluded so as not to spend again on those who already bought.

cards.q: The owner also wants to prospect new customers similar to their best buyers. What role does their custom audience play there?
options: None, custom audiences are only for remarketing
It serves as a seed to create a lookalike
cards.explanation: A quality custom audience serves as a seed for a prospecting lookalike, in addition to its use in remarketing and exclusion.

cards.q: Custom audience
cards.a: People with a prior relationship to the business: web, app, customer list, engagement.

cards.q: Custom audience sources
cards.a: Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement.

cards.q: Triple use of custom audiences
cards.a: Remarketing, lookalike seed, and exclusion of those who already converted.

cards.q: How is a customer list uploaded?
cards.a: Hashed (emails/phones protected) before the match.

cards.body: A lookalike audience is the tool for prospecting at scale without shooting blindly. It starts from a seed (a quality custom audience you use as a base, such as your best customers) and asks Meta to find people with a similar profile who don't know you yet. The logic is simple: if Meta understands what traits your most valuable customers share, it can go out and look for more people with those same traits. That's why this concept unites the module's two worlds: it takes your own signal (a custom audience) and uses it to fuel prospecting toward a new audience. For example, an artisanal jewelry brand called Plata Viva takes its recurring-buyer list as a seed and creates a lookalike to reach people with similar tastes and behaviors who have never bought from it.

When creating a lookalike you choose a percentage from 1% to 10% that defines the balance between precision and reach. 1. Lookalike 1%: takes the 1% of the country's population most similar to your seed; it's the most precise, but with the lowest reach. 2. Lookalike 5–10%: greatly expands reach, but by including more people the similarity to the seed gets diluted. There's no "correct" number: 1% is a good fit when you prioritize quality and 10% when you need volume to scale. Also, the lookalike is always generated over a target country: it's built from the population of the country where you want to prospect, so you need to create one per market. For example, a fintech called Pago Ágil that only operates in Mexico creates its lookalike 1% over Mexico to get as close as possible to its best users.

A lookalike is never better than the seed it starts from: if the seed is poor, the result will be too. This is known as garbage in, garbage out: the algorithm can only copy the pattern you give it. A seed of high-value customers produces a much stronger lookalike than one of mere visitors, because Meta learns to look for people similar to those who actually bring you money. The size of the seed also matters: the recommendation is between 1,000 and 50,000 people; with too few there isn't enough of a pattern and with too many what they have in common gets diluted. For example, a boutique gym called Fuerza Urbana will make a better lookalike with its seed of 3,000 active higher-spend members than with a list of 80,000 casual site visitors.

Just like custom audiences, lookalikes are created in the audiences section, not inside the ad set. In Ads Manager, open the menu and go to Audiences; click Create audience and choose Lookalike Audience. Meta will ask you for three things: the source (or seed: the custom audience you'll use as a base), the target country or region, and the size as a percentage (1% to 10%). You can create several sizes at once. Once generated, the lookalike is available to use as targeting when building your prospecting ad set.

rows: The better the seed (high-value customers), the better the lookalike
More similar to the seed, more precise, less reach
The lookalike is generated by target country

cards.label: SEED QUALITY
cards.body: A seed of high-value customers produces better lookalikes than one of mere visitors. Recommended seed size: ~1,000–50,000 people.

cards.label: COMMON TRAP
cards.body: A poor seed (all visitors, with no value filter) generates a weak lookalike. Garbage in, garbage out: the quality of the lookalike depends on the quality of the seed.

cards.q: You want maximum precision to prospect people similar to your best customers. Which configuration?
options: Lookalike 1% over a seed of high-value customers
cards.explanation: Maximum precision = lookalike 1% (most similar to the seed) and a quality seed (high-value customers). 10% expands but dilutes the similarity; interests don't use your own signal.

cards.q: An audience of people similar to a quality seed is called a ___ audience.
cards.explanation: Lookalike audience: it starts from a seed (a quality custom audience) and looks for similar profiles; it serves for prospecting.

cards.q: What is the difference between a lookalike 1% and a 10%?
options: 1% is more similar and precise with less reach; 10% more reach and less similar
They are identical, only the name changes
cards.explanation: 1% = more similar to the seed, more precise, less reach. 5–10% = more reach, less similar.

cards.q: What improves a lookalike most is the quality of the ___.
cards.explanation: A quality seed (e.g., high-value customers) produces better lookalikes than one of mere visitors.

cards.q: The lookalike is generated mainly based on:
cards.explanation: The lookalike is generated by target country from the seed.

cards.q: Recommended seed size for a good lookalike:
cards.explanation: The recommended seed size is ~1,000–50,000 people, ideally high-value.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: A B2B SaaS for electronic invoicing with a good volume of paying customers wants to prospect new companies similar to its best accounts. It has a custom audience of high-value customers (~8,000 people) and operates mainly in Mexico.

cards.q: Which lookalike configuration gives maximum precision to resemble its best accounts?
options: Lookalike 1% over the seed of high-value customers
cards.explanation: Maximum precision = lookalike 1% over a quality seed (high-value customers). 10% expands but dilutes the similarity.

cards.q: Is the seed of ~8,000 high-value customers adequate?
options: No, it's too small for any lookalike
Yes, it's within the recommended range (~1,000–50,000) and is high-value
It only works for remarketing, never for a lookalike
It must be reduced to 50 people
cards.explanation: ~8,000 high-value people is within the recommended seed range (~1,000–50,000) and improves the lookalike's quality.

cards.q: They operate in Mexico. How does that affect the lookalike?
options: The lookalike is generated by target country, so it's created for Mexico
The country doesn't affect the lookalike
The seed must be created in another country
cards.explanation: The lookalike is generated by target country: to prospect in Mexico, the lookalike is created over that country.

cards.q: Lookalike audience
cards.a: People similar to a seed (a quality custom audience); for prospecting.

cards.q: Lookalike 1% vs 10%
cards.a: 1% = more similar and precise, less reach. 10% = more reach, less similar.

cards.q: What makes a lookalike better?
cards.a: A quality seed (e.g., high-value customers).

cards.q: How is the lookalike generated by geography?
cards.a: By target country; recommended seed size ~1,000–50,000 people.

cards.label: INTEGRATIVE CASE
cards.body: An ecommerce with good sales volume and a solid Pixel + CAPI. It wants to scale acquisition without cannibalizing current customers, and at the same time recover abandoned carts.

cards.q: What audience architecture do you propose?
options: Advantage+ Audience / lookalike 1% of high-value buyers for prospecting, excluding current customers; remarketing separately
A single ad set with 12 interests
cards.explanation: Scaling acquisition without cannibalizing = prospecting with Advantage+/high-value lookalike excluding customers, and a separate remarketing effort. Remarketing only doesn't scale; 12 interests over-segments.

cards.q: How do you avoid paying twice for the same user in this plan?
options: By excluding the current-customer custom audiences from the prospecting campaign
cards.explanation: You exclude the customer/buyer custom audiences in prospecting so as not to pay again for the same user.

cards.q: Why does 'remarketing to buyers only' NOT meet the objective?
options: Because it doesn't scale acquisition: it only re-engages those you already know, it doesn't bring new customers
Because the Pixel doesn't allow it
Because it's more expensive than Reservation
cards.explanation: Remarketing re-engages known people; to scale acquisition you need prospecting (Advantage+/lookalike) toward a new audience.

cards.q: For cart recovery, what custom audience do you define?
options: Website custom audience by AddToCart, excluding Purchase
cards.explanation: Abandoned cart = Website custom audience by AddToCart excluding those who already fired Purchase.

lessons.title: Match the key concepts of Module 4
lessons.instruction: Tap the term and then its definition. Each mistake costs 1 ❤️.
pairs: Default: signals as a suggestion, Meta finds conversions
Manual targeting with strict control of the audience
Narrows by interests, behaviors, and demographics
Meta goes beyond the interest if it improves results
Tiny audience: high cost, little delivery
People with a prior relationship to the business
Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement
Your own list uploaded hashed for the match
Similar to a seed; for prospecting at scale
More similar and precise, less reach

lessons.title: Order the audience build by funnel
lessons.instruction: The planner builds the audiences in this order. Reorder with ▲▼ and verify.
items: Build custom audiences from your sources (web, app, list, engagement)
Create a lookalike 1% using the best custom audience as a seed
Prospect with Advantage+ Audience / lookalike, excluding current customers
Remarket to custom audiences (e.g., abandoned carts)
Exclude buyers so as not to pay twice
lessons.explanation: First you build your own signal (custom), the lookalike seed comes from that, then prospecting excluding customers and remarketing separately.

cards.q: The audience default recommended by Meta is ___.
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Audience: signals as a suggestion.

cards.q: With hard legal restrictions you use the ___ audience.
cards.explanation: Only the original audience imposes a real limit.

cards.q: Narrowing by interests, behaviors, and demographics is ___ targeting.
cards.explanation: Detailed targeting.

cards.q: Over-segmenting creates tiny audiences and the cost ___.
cards.explanation: With no room to optimize, the cost spikes.

cards.q: A customer list is uploaded ___ before the match.
cards.explanation: Hashed: emails/phones protected.

cards.q: A lookalike starts from a ___ (a quality custom audience).
cards.explanation: The seed determines the quality of the lookalike.

cards.q: The lookalike ___% is the most similar to the seed.
cards.explanation: 1% = maximum similarity, less reach.

cards.q: With no legal restrictions and a conversion signal, which audience do you start with?
options: Advantage+ Audience giving it signals as a suggestion
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Audience is the recommended default when there's signal and no legal limits.

cards.q: When the original audience?
options: With legal/brand restrictions or strict control
cards.explanation: When a hard audience limit is needed.

cards.q: In Advantage+ Audience the signals you add are:
cards.explanation: They prioritize; the system can expand.

cards.q: Detailed targeting narrows by:
cards.explanation: Interests, behaviors, and demographics.

cards.q: Risk of over-targeting:
options: Tiny audience: high cost, little delivery
cards.explanation: With no room to optimize, the cost goes up.

cards.q: The four custom audience sources:
options: Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement
cards.explanation: Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement.

cards.q: A customer list is uploaded:
cards.explanation: Hashed, for the match without exposing data.

cards.q: Uses of a custom audience:
cards.explanation: Remarketing, seed, and exclusion of those who already converted.

cards.q: Lookalike 1% vs 10%:
cards.explanation: 1% = more similar, less reach.

cards.q: What improves a lookalike most?
cards.explanation: A quality seed (high-value) = a better lookalike.

cards.q: The lookalike is generated by:
cards.explanation: By target country, from the seed.

cards.q: Scaling acquisition without cannibalizing customers:
options: Advantage+/lookalike prospecting excluding customers; remarketing separately
cards.explanation: Prospecting excluding customers + separate remarketing.

questions.q: Is Advantage+ Audience the recommended default?
questions.explanation: Yes, when there's signal and no legal limits.

questions.q: Are the signals in Advantage+ Audience a hard limit?
questions.explanation: They prioritize; the system can expand.
A tiny audience: the system doesn't optimize.

questions.q: Is a customer list uploaded hashed?
Can a custom audience be a lookalike seed?
questions.explanation: It's one of its three uses.

questions.q: Does a lookalike 1% have more reach than 10%?
Does the quality of the lookalike depend on the seed?
questions.explanation: A poor seed = a weak lookalike.

questions.q: Is remarketing enough to scale acquisition?

cards.q: Advantage+ Audience
cards.a: Recommended default: you provide signals as a suggestion and Meta finds who converts, without hard limits.

cards.q: When the original (manual) audience?
cards.a: When there are legal/brand restrictions or strict control of the audience is needed.

cards.q: In Advantage+ Audience, are custom audiences a limit?
cards.a: No; they work as suggestions that prioritize, not as a rigid limit.

cards.q: When is Advantage+ Audience a good fit?
cards.a: To maximize results, prospect, and when a conversion signal is available.

cards.q: Detailed targeting
cards.a: Narrows by interests, behaviors, and demographics; useful for cold audiences without your own signal.

cards.q: Narrow / exclude
cards.a: Refines the audience with AND/NOT to fine-tune it.

cards.q: Detailed targeting expansion
cards.a: Lets Meta go beyond the interest if it improves results (in the original audience).

cards.q: Risk of over-targeting
cards.a: Audience too small: the system doesn't optimize, cost goes up and delivery drops.

cards.q: Custom audience
cards.a: People with a prior relationship to the business: web, app, customer list, engagement.

cards.q: Custom audience sources
cards.a: Website, App activity, Customer list, Engagement.

cards.q: Triple use of custom audiences
cards.a: Remarketing, lookalike seed, and exclusion of those who already converted.

cards.q: How is a customer list uploaded?
cards.a: Hashed (emails/phones protected) before the match.

cards.q: Lookalike audience
cards.a: People similar to a seed (a quality custom audience); for prospecting.

cards.q: Lookalike 1% vs 10%
cards.a: 1% = more similar and precise, less reach. 10% = more reach, less similar.

cards.q: What makes a lookalike better?
cards.a: A quality seed (e.g., high-value customers).

cards.q: How is the lookalike generated by geography?
cards.a: By target country; recommended seed size ~1,000–50,000 people.

cards.q: Recover abandoned carts
cards.a: Website custom audience by AddToCart, excluding Purchase.

cards.q: Scale acquisition without cannibalizing
cards.a: Advantage+/lookalike prospecting excluding customers; remarketing separately.

cards.q: Does remarketing scale acquisition?
cards.a: No; it only re-engages known people. New customers require prospecting.

cards.q: Broad audience with a good signal
cards.a: Tends to perform better than an over-segmented one.

cards.q: Poor seed
cards.a: Generates a weak lookalike: garbage in, garbage out.
